content:
Key Findings

  • Chorley Borough Council's total analysed spending across five financial years, from 2021/22 to 2025/26, amounts to £372.2M.
  • The department "GOVN - Policy & Governance" represents the largest expenditure, totalling £245.1M, which is 65.9% of the council's overall spending.
  • "Policy & Governance" is the second highest spending area, with an outlay of £39.1M, accounting for 10.5% of the total.
  • Combined, the top two "Policy & Governance" related categories make up over three-quarters of the council's expenditure, at £284.2M.
  • "Communities", one of the lowest spending categories, accounted for £3.1M, or 0.8% of the total analysed spend.

Chorley's Financial Landscape: An Overview of £372.2M Spending

Chorley Borough Council has seen significant financial activity over the five financial years from 2021/22 to 2025/26. An analysis of its published spending data reveals a total expenditure of £372.2M. This comprehensive figure provides a snapshot of how the district council allocates its resources across various services and departments. Understanding these allocations is crucial for residents to gauge the council's priorities and operational focus.

The data, drawn from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code, offers a detailed look into the council's financial commitments. While the total sum is substantial, the distribution of this spending across different departments highlights specific areas of focus. This article will delve into these departmental expenditures, identifying where the majority of funds are directed and which services receive comparatively less investment.

Dominant Spending: Policy and Governance at the Forefront

A striking aspect of Chorley Borough Council's spending is the overwhelming proportion allocated to policy and governance functions. The department "GOVN - Policy & Governance" stands out with an expenditure of £245.1M. This single category accounts for a significant 65.9% of the council's entire analysed spending. Such a high concentration suggests a substantial investment in strategic direction, democratic services, and the overarching management of the council's operations.

Complementing this, the category simply named "Policy & Governance" represents the second largest spending area, with £39.1M. This equates to 10.5% of the total budget. When combined, these two governance-related categories total £284.2M, making up over three-quarters of the council's expenditure. This indicates a strong emphasis on the foundational and strategic elements of council administration, potentially encompassing everything from council leadership and legal services to financial oversight and democratic processes.

Key Service Areas and Their Allocations

Beyond the dominant governance categories, other essential services receive notable, albeit smaller, portions of the council's budget. "CUST - Customer & Digital" is the third largest spending department, with an outlay of £21.5M, representing 5.8% of the total. This suggests investment in improving resident interactions, digital platforms, and technology infrastructure. Following closely is "GROW - Commercial & Property", which spent £19.6M, or 5.3% of the total. This category likely covers the council's commercial ventures, property management, and economic development initiatives.

Further down the list, "GROW - Commercial Services" accounts for £12.1M, or 3.2% of the total spend. This could include a range of services aimed at supporting local businesses or generating council income. "EARL - Communities" spent £11.4M, making up 3.1% of the budget, indicating investment in local community projects and support. Another "Commercial & Property" category shows an expenditure of £10.5M, or 2.8%. These figures demonstrate a diversified approach to service delivery, balancing core administrative functions with direct community and economic support.

Detailed Departmental Spending Breakdown

To provide a clearer picture of Chorley Borough Council's spending priorities, the following table details the top ten departments by expenditure. This allows for a direct comparison of how funds are distributed across different areas, from strategic governance to more localised services. The figures highlight the significant disparity between the highest and lowest spending departments.

Rank Department Amount % of Total
1 GOVN - Policy & Governance £245.1M 65.9%
2 Policy & Governance £39.1M 10.5%
3 CUST - Customer & Digital £21.5M 5.8%
4 GROW - Commercial & Property £19.6M 5.3%
5 GROW - Commercial Services £12.1M 3.2%
6 EARL - Communities £11.4M 3.1%
7 Commercial & Property £10.5M 2.8%
8 PLAN - Planning & Development £5.9M 1.6%
9 Customer & Digital £3.5M 0.9%
10 Communities £3.1M 0.8%

The table clearly illustrates the concentration of spending within governance, followed by customer services, commercial activities, and community support. "PLAN - Planning & Development" is ranked eighth, with an expenditure of £5.9M, representing 1.6% of the total. This department is crucial for local development and regulatory oversight. The two lowest listed categories are "Customer & Digital" at £3.5M (0.9%) and "Communities" at £3.1M (0.8%). These figures, while smaller in comparison, still represent significant investments in vital public services.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Chorley Borough Council about the specific breakdown of spending within the "GOVN - Policy & Governance" categories.
  • Attend council committee meetings, as they are open to the public, to hear discussions on budget allocations and departmental performance.
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see details of major contracts awarded and the services they cover.
  • Write to your local councillor about the significant proportion of spending allocated to policy and governance versus direct community services.
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data for Chorley Borough Council and other Lancashire councils yourself.
This article is based on Chorley Borough Council's published spending data covering 2021/22 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
